children.name: Mammalia
children.description: Warm-blooded vertebrates with hair or fur. Young are typically born live and nourished with milk produced by the mother. The class includes over 6,400 species ranging from bats and whales to primates and hoofed animals.

children.name: Carnivora
children.description: Despite the name, Carnivora is defined by shared ancestry and skull anatomy, not diet. The order includes dedicated meat-eaters like cats and wolves, but also omnivores like bears and raccoons, and even the bamboo-eating giant panda.

children.name: Perissodactyla
children.description: Hoofed mammals that bear weight on an odd number of toes. Horses walk on a single toe per foot — the hoof is a highly modified toenail. Rhinoceroses and tapirs retain three. Counterintuitively, this makes horses the most "odd-toed" of all.

children.name: Cetacea
children.description: Fully aquatic mammals descended from land-dwelling artiodactyls, most closely related to hippos. About 90 living species divided into Mysticeti (baleen whales) and Odontoceti (toothed whales). The blue whale is the largest animal known to have ever existed.

children.name: Artiodactyla
children.description: Hoofed mammals that bear weight on an even number of toes (2 or 4). The most species-rich ungulate order, including cattle, deer, pigs, camels, and giraffes. Many are ruminants with multi-chambered stomachs for digesting tough plant material.

children.name: Caprinae
children.taxonomicNote: Subfamily of Bovidae (tribe Caprini), treated here as a family-level group for consistency.

children.name: Bovidae
children.taxonomicNote: Bovinae subfamily — cattle, buffalo, and bison. Caprinae (sheep & goats) is treated separately.

children.name: Lagomorpha
children.description: Small herbivorous mammals with continuously growing incisors and a second pair of small peg teeth behind the upper incisors. Unlike rodents, lagomorphs have four upper incisors. They are highly successful colonisers found on every continent except Antarctica.

children.name: Primates
children.description: The order that includes humans and their closest relatives. Divided into Strepsirrhini (wet-nosed primates: lemurs, lorises) and Haplorhini (dry-nosed: tarsiers, monkeys, apes). Primates are characterised by forward-facing eyes, grasping hands, and relatively large brains. Around 500 living species.

children.name: Platyrrhini
children.taxonomicNote: Platyrrhini parvorder — treated here as a family-level group spanning Cebidae, Callitrichidae, Atelidae, and Pitheciidae.

children.name: Diprotodontia
children.description: The largest order of marsupials, containing kangaroos, wallabies, possums, wombats, and koalas. Diprotodontia are united by fused lower incisors (hence the name, 'two front teeth') and a syndactylous second and third toe. With over 140 species, they represent the dominant large land mammals of Australasia.

children.name: Aves
children.description: Feathered, warm-blooded vertebrates descended from theropod dinosaurs. All living birds have beaks, lay hard-shelled eggs, and are endothermic. With around 10,000 species they are the most diverse group of land vertebrates.

children.name: Galliformes
children.description: Heavy-bodied, ground-dwelling birds built for short bursts of flight rather than sustained soaring. Galliformes gave us almost all domestic poultry — chickens, turkeys, guinea fowl, and quail — and include the spectacular peafowl and pheasants.

children.name: Anseriformes
children.description: Aquatic birds characterised by webbed feet, broad flat bills, and dense waterproofed plumage. The order includes ducks, geese, and swans, along with the more primitive screamers of South America. Many species undertake long seasonal migrations.

children.name: Piciformes
children.description: Mostly tree-climbing birds with zygodactyl feet (two toes forward, two back) and stiffened tail feathers used as a prop against bark. Woodpeckers drum on resonant dead wood to establish territory and excavate nest cavities that later shelter dozens of other species — they are among the most important keystone cavity-nesters in forest ecosystems.

children.name: Columbiformes
children.description: Plump, round-headed birds with small bills and a rapid, direct flight. Columbiformes are among the most successful bird orders, inhabiting every continent except Antarctica. They feed on seeds and fruit, drink by sucking water rather than tilting the head back, and produce crop milk to feed their young — a trait otherwise found only in flamingos and some penguins.

children.name: Passeriformes
children.description: The largest order of birds, containing over 6,000 species — more than half of all bird species on Earth. Passerines are defined by a specialised foot with three toes pointing forward and one back, locking automatically when the bird perches. The order includes the songbirds, whose complex vocalisations are produced by a uniquely elaborate syrinx.

children.name: Chondrichthyes
children.description: Fish with skeletons made entirely of cartilage rather than bone. The class includes sharks, rays, and chimaeras — over 1,100 species. Their evolutionary lineage stretches back more than 450 million years, making them among the oldest jawed vertebrates.

children.name: Carcharhiniformes
children.description: The largest order of sharks with around 270 species. Ground sharks are characterised by a nictitating membrane over the eye, two dorsal fins without spines, an anal fin, and five gill slits. The order includes many of the most familiar and ecologically important species.

children.name: Lamniformes
children.description: Fifteen species of powerful, largely pelagic sharks including the great white, makos, and basking shark. Lamniformes are distinguished by their large mouths extending behind the eyes and lack of a nictitating membrane. Several species are endothermic, maintaining body temperature above the surrounding water.

children.name: Orectolobiformes
children.description: Around 45 species of bottom-dwelling and reef-associated sharks, plus the whale shark — the largest fish on Earth. Carpet sharks are typically flattened with cryptic patterning, though the filter-feeding whale shark and nurse sharks are notable exceptions. Most are confined to the Indo-Pacific.

children.name: Tardigrada
children.description: Microscopic eight-legged animals found in every habitat on Earth — from deep ocean trenches to Himalayan glaciers, from Antarctic ice to city roof gutters. Tardigrades are the most resilient animals known: in a desiccated state called cryptobiosis they can withstand temperatures approaching absolute zero, pressures six times that of the deepest ocean, ionising radiation 1,000 times the lethal dose for a human, and the complete vacuum of outer space. Around 1,300 species are described, most under half a millimetre long.
